dict.string:
Hither sync — daily DSM share enumeration + autofs map regeneration in user GUI context.
Purpose: Fires once daily at 04:23 local time. Calls the DSM Web API as TARGET_USER, enumerates the SMB-readable shares for that user, renders an /etc/hither_{nas} indirect map body, diffs it against the on-disk file, and (when changed) pipes it through `sudo -n /usr/local/sbin/hither-write-map {nas}` which atomically writes the map and runs `automount -cv`. Idempotent — re-runs are no-ops when the share list and on-disk map agree.
Context: Runs as a LaunchAgent under the user's GUI session — NOT a LaunchDaemon. The user-context requirement is load-bearing: DSM password lookup via `security find-internet-password` requires Keychain access, which is only available in a GUI-session security context. The same Keychain entry that Finder Cmd-K uses for SMB mounts is the one this agent reads. Running this work as root via LaunchDaemon was tried and rejected (Keychain access fails under launchd-root with errSecAuthFailed -25293).
Dependencies: requires /usr/local/libexec/hither/hither-sync.sh (root-owned, mode 0755 — installed by `hither bootstrap`); /usr/local/sbin/hither-write-map (root-owned, mode 0755); /etc/sudoers.d/hither-write-map granting this user NOPASSWD on the wrapper for the configured NAS hostnames; a Keychain entry for each NAS in NAS_LIST keyed (server=$NAS, account=$TARGET_USER). Network reachability to the NAS (LAN or Tailscale) at fire time.
Operation: stdout → ~/Library/Logs/hither/sync.log; stderr → ~/Library/Logs/hither/sync.stderr.log. Use `launchctl print gui/$(id -u)/com.johnrandall.hither.sync` to inspect state. Use `launchctl kickstart gui/$(id -u)/com.johnrandall.hither.sync` to fire manually (or `hither sync` for the same effect from a user shell). Use `launchctl bootout gui/$(id -u)/com.johnrandall.hither.sync` to disable.
Sibling: com.johnrandall.hither.bootstrap (LaunchDaemon, root context, defends /etc/synthetic.conf + /etc/auto_master against macOS update reverts). The two daemons have non-overlapping roles — defender does local file repair only (no network), sync does the network/API/credential work (no file repair). Splitting them by privilege/context is what makes Hither self-contained on a single Mac.
References: github.com/johntrandall/hither; admin-technical ADR-NNN (Hither lazy-mounter); design-decisions.md "Why a LaunchAgent for sync, not a LaunchDaemon".
